title: Fine-grained latency analysis of real-world 5G-enabled multi-tier edge computing for school zone traffic safety - Discover Internet of Things
text:
Article summary
AI generated
As urbanization increases, ensuring traffic safety in sensitive areas like school zones has become a pressing challenge, necessitating innovative real-time monitoring systems. This study successfully evaluated the 5-Safe multi-tier 5G edge computing system, revealing critical insights into its performance, including average latencies below 58 ms but occasional spikes up to 800 ms, thereby highlighting both the potential and limitations of current technologies in meeting stringent real-time traffic management requirements.
Abstract
This article presents a real-world evaluation of 5-Safe, an innovative multi-tier 5G edge computing system designed to enhance traffic safety in school zones. The study addresses the critical challenge of implementing reliable and low-latency communication, data processing, and decision-making in complex urban environments. We employ a diverse methodology, including User Datagram Protocol (UDP) transmission analysis, Graphic Processing Unit (GPU) evaluation, and comparative studies of the messaging protocol Message Queueing Telemetry Transport (MQTT) and the Apache Kafka streaming platform. Our research revealed significant insights into system performance, such as latency variability in UDP transmissions, consistent GPU processing efficiency, and the superior performance of Kafka in data transmission. End-to-end system latency measurements highlighted both the potential and limitations of current edge computing technologies in meeting real-time traffic management requirements. We observe that average latencies remain below 58 ms, while maximum spikes reach up to 800 ms, surpassing typical thresholds (e.g.,<100 ms) required for preemptive traffic alerts in pedestrian zones. These findings contribute valuable benchmarks for future research and development in smart city applications. The significance of the study lies in its analysis in real-world implementation, which offers critical insights into the challenges and opportunities of developing responsive, reliable traffic monitoring systems. Our results pave the way for advancements in edge computing architectures and communication protocols, with broad implications for the design of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) requiring precise, low-latency data processing in dynamic urban environments.
1 Introduction
In an era of increasing urbanization and technological advancement, ensuring traffic safety, particularly in sensitive areas like school zones, has become a critical challenge for modern societies. The complex and dynamic nature of urban traffic environments and the vulnerability of school-age pedestrians require innovative approaches to real-time monitoring and rapid response systems. However, there is limited understanding of how advanced, real-time traffic safety solutions perform when deployed over common, commercially available 5G telecommunication networks, which are shared with other users and subject to dynamic spectrum allocation and varying network loads. Existing safety-critical applications such as emergency vehicle warnings or pedestrian alerts, often require end-to-end latencies below 100 ms, with some contexts demanding under 50 ms for hard real-time responsiveness. This study evaluates whether the 5-Safe system meets these operational thresholds. The 5-Safe project addresses a significant gap in current traffic safety solutions: the lack of adequate real-time monitoring studies covering swift identification and mitigation of potential hazards in school zones under the described limited conditions.
Most previous studies have evaluated advanced traffic safety solutions using dedicated 5G campus networks or in highly controlled environments, which do not accurately represent the challenges of real-world deployment. In contrast, our study is among the first to investigate the performance and reliability of a real-time traffic safety system in a live school zone using the existing, shared 5G telecommunication infrastructure in Landshut. This setup is subject to the typical variability and constraints of public networks, providing critical insights into the practical feasibility and scalability of such systems in everyday urban settings.
Our primary objective is to create a comprehensive monitoring system that seamlessly integrates data from multiple sensor types, including high-resolution cameras and Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) sensors, to provide a holistic view of the traffic environment. By leveraging advanced edge computing techniques, we aim to process this multi-modal data stream in real-time, enabling rapid detection of potential hazards, accurate tracking of vehicles and pedestrians, and immediate implementation of safety measures.
Our contributions can be summarized as follows:
-
We present an architecture that combines multi-tier edge computing with sophisticated sensor fusion techniques, allowing for distributed processing of high-volume data streams using the existing, shared 5G telecommunication infrastructure in Landshut.
-
We apply algorithms for real-time object detection, tracking, and decision-making optimized explicitly for the unique challenges of school zone environments.
-
We provide comprehensive empirical studies on the performance of the proposed real-world deployment, offering valuable insights into the practical implementation of complex real-time safety systems in urban traffic environments based on real-world experiments.
The remainder of this article is structured as follows. Section 2 provides an overview of related work in traffic safety systems and real-time data processing, highlighting the current state of the art and identifying gaps that our research addresses. Section 3 details the system architecture of our 5-Safe project, explaining the multi-tier edge computing model and the integration of various sensors and processing units. Section 4 presents our methodology and experimental studies, outlining the approach taken to evaluate the system performance and the specific experiments conducted. Section 5 outlines the experimental setup and results, providing a comprehensive analysis of the system’s effectiveness in terms of latency, accuracy, and overall performance in real-world scenarios. Sections 5.3 and 6 discuss and conclude the article’s findings, proposing directions for future work.
2 Related work
In the following, we review the existing intelligent infrastructure approaches for real-time traffic systems. In addition, we present the existing real-world measurement investigation in the edge computing environment.
2.1 Intelligent infrastructure projects
Over the last decades, research projects have been conducted to enhance road infrastructure with sensors and communication systems capable of broadcasting real-time traffic information. An early project, PRE-DRIVE C2X, launched in 2008, aimed to establish a European vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication network. However, significant progress in intelligent infrastructure initiatives was not observed until around 2019. Around this time, the Test Area Autonomous Driving Baden-Württemberg was established, featuring intelligent infrastructure at a Karlsruhe, Germany, intersection equipped with two cameras and integrated with traffic lights to detect and track road users, sharing data online via V2X communication [1].
In 2019, researchers installed 16-channel LiDAR sensors and communication devices at three intersections in Reno, Nevada, USA. Junxuan Zhao et al. employed multiple sensors positioned at different intersection corners to address occlusion issues [2]. In Ulm, Germany, Robert Bosch GmbH and Karlsruhe Institute of Technology launched MEC-View in 2019, utilizing cameras and LiDAR to study how integrating on-board and stationary sensor data improves the detection and tracking of road users [3].
In 2020, RWTH Aachen University deployed up to a hundred 64-channel LiDAR sensors at a German intersection to assess the advantage of fused point clouds for multi-object detection and tracking [4].
Providentia, an intelligent infrastructure project near Munich, Germany, equips gantry bridges on the A9 highway with cameras and radars to construct a digital twin of traffic, a live digital model using multi-modal sensor data, primarily to improve the perception of autonomous vehicles. This digital twin also serves in traffic flow management and wrong-way driver alerts. The project successor, Providentia++, extends traffic monitoring to rural roads and an urban intersection, incorporating LiDAR into the sensor suite [5].
Unlike the aforementioned projects, our 5-Safe real deployment [6] offers a real-world setup and explores innovative technology combinations to enhance safety for children on their way to school. A network of multi-modal sensors and devices is installed around two schools in Landshut, Germany. In conjunction with the 5G communication, edge computing infrastructure, and machine learning for sensor data processing, this setup aims to predict potentially critical situations and actively warn road users.
2.2 Real-world edge computing measurement
Yong et al. [7] present a comprehensive long-term, city-wide measurement study focused on wireless access latency between moving vehicles and Fog Computing systems. Based on their findings, they introduce a novel framework, AdaptiveFog, designed to enable vehicles to dynamically alternate between Mobile Network Operators (MNO) networks and Fog/Cloud servers. This adaptive approach optimizes latency performance within LTE-based Fog Computing environments.
Milad et al. [8] present a cognitive service orchestration framework for 5G edge computing, addressing limitations in existing orchestration methods that overlook real-time network conditions such as OpenELB [9]. By incorporating reinforcement learning, the framework is dynamically adapted to optimize latency for delay-sensitive applications. The evaluation with a smart city object detection use case shows a 58% latency reduction compared to OpenELB, demonstrating its effectiveness in complex 5G environments.
Pardis et al. [10] introduce a real-time passenger counting system for bus stops in smart cities, implemented on Nvidia Edge devices powered by solar panels. The system prioritizes privacy and cost-efficiency, adapting and optimizing computer vision models specifically for edge computing. Using an overhead fisheye lens dataset, YOLO [11] outperformed other models and was deployed via Nvidia DeepStream [12], achieving over 50% reduction in GPU, Central Processing Unit (CPU), and memory usage. This work offers a more accurate, energy-efficient, and ethically responsible solution to count passengers at smart city bus stops.
In a prior work, we investigated offload latency for time-critical applications in 5G-based edge computing in [13]. Accordingly, we proposed a layered offloading framework using Docker [14], Kubernetes [15], and checkpointing [16] to perform real-world measurements of latency when offloading tasks from devices, such as vehicles, to nearby edge nodes. This prior experimental study has shown that proactive arrangements at the edge nodes significantly reduce offloading time. Furthermore, we examined various offloading strategies within vehicular edge computing, highlighting the effectiveness of the framework for latency-sensitive applications in 5G networks.
Although these efforts have examined transmission latency, energy consumption, and service responsiveness, this real-world study adds complexity by considering the transmission latency of streaming data, optimizing execution latency at the edge, and focusing on maintaining the system QoS to guarantee service responsiveness and ensure timely output messages for traffic signs.
In contrast to earlier deployments, our analysis offers more rigorous latency profiling. Moreover, our work differs from foundational efforts such as ETSI MEC standardization on latency-aware edge computing, DSRC-based school zone alert systems, and connected vehicle platforms such as CV2X, 5G-CARMEN, and 5GCroCo. These initiatives have provided groundwork for mobile edge support in vehicular environments. However, 5-Safe addresses the real-world deployment feasibility over public 5G networks and explores data fusion and end-to-end timing guarantees in dense school zones, which are currently underexplored in existing literature. We also note that the Google Intersection Safety Analytics platform, while effective, focuses on aggregate statistical modeling and historical trend analysis rather than real-time edge-based actuation.
While existing projects such as MEC-View, Providentia, and RWTH Aachen showcase advanced infrastructure setups, direct latency or system reliability comparisons are rarely reported. In this work, we present 24-h real-time profiling and performance benchmarking under operational constraints, offering a unique basis for comparative evaluations in future deployments.
3 System architecture
Our 5-Safe system integrates multi-source data collection, real-time processing, and automated decision-making to enhance traffic safety near schools. The system architecture, as illustrated in Fig. 1, combines camera and LiDAR sensors with a multi-tier edge computing infrastructure, enabling comprehensive environmental monitoring, data fusion, and timely decision-making (Fig. 2).
The data collection forms the foundation of our system, utilizing two distinct mono-modal sensor arrays. Five LiDAR sensors and five high-resolution cameras provide a comprehensive view of the monitored area.
In parallel, each LiDAR is synchronized via GPS time and configured to send raw point cloud data at 5 frames per second. This data is transmitted over UDP through a Network Connector to a near-edge server equipped with GPU resources. The near-edge handles real-time object detection and tracking for each LiDAR stream. A Sync component for LiDAR aligns point clouds using GPS timestamps, and the Fusion component for LiDAR integrates the detections across the five LiDARs into a consistent fused object list. This list is then forwarded to the cloud-edge.
Each camera is connected to a Local Processing Unit (LPU) equipped with a Jetson module [17]. These LPUs are synchronized via an NTP server and perform local object detection. Each unit generates object lists containing detected entities (vehicles, pedestrians, etc.) with location and timestamp data. These object lists are transmitted to the Cloud-Edge over a 5G router configured with a VPN profile (Network Connector). Upon arrival, the data streams, managed via Apache Kafka [18], are each assigned to a unique topic and processed independently by Bird’s Eye View (BEV) tracking containers.
Although termed ’AI-based’ in earlier drafts, the system currently employs pretrained deterministic object detection models (YOLO-based object detection) without online adaptation or learning. Future extensions may incorporate adaptive models with real-time fine-tuning loops based on edge feedback.
At the cloud-edge, a dedicated Sync component for cameras aligns object lists temporally, using Camera 1 as a reference. Based on the time differences and average class-specific speeds, spatial corrections are applied. Following synchronization, the Fusion component for cameras merges the corrected object lists into a unified multi-camera spatial model of the monitored environment.
At the cloud-edge, a Decision-Making container processes both fused camera and LiDAR outputs to assess traffic conditions and generate appropriate safety actions, such as speed reduction warnings. The communication between various components primarily relies on Kafka. Yet, the resulting decision is published to a Kafka topic and routed through a Kafka–MQTT proxy, which forwards the message to the MQTT broker. This proxy architecture was chosen because the variable message signs only support MQTT subscriptions; thus, Kafka messages must be bridged for compatibility. Performance profiling during deployment indicated that this bridging introduces negligible overhead (<15 ms), maintaining compliance with real-time constraints. Variable traffic signs and system health monitoring, which subscribe via the MQTT messaging protocol [19], display the decision in real time to influence driver behavior and enhance safety in the school zone.
To ensure robust operation and enable proactive maintenance, the 5-Safe system incorporates a comprehensive monitoring and communication infrastructure. System performance is continuously monitored using tools such as Jaeger [20] for distributed tracing, Grafana [21] for visualization, and Prometheus [22] for metrics collection and alerting.
4 Methodology and experimental studies
This section presents an overview of the research approach and experimental investigations conducted to evaluate and validate the 5-Safe system. It is divided into two main parts: Methodology and Experimental Studies. The Methodology subsection outlines the overall research design, data collection methods, and analytical techniques employed in our study. The Experimental Studies subsection details specific experiments conducted to assess various aspects of the system’s performance, including data transmission, processing efficiency, and overall effectiveness in enhancing traffic safety near schools.
4.1 Methodology
Our research methodology for the 5-Safe project employs a multi-faceted approach combining theoretical analysis, system design, and empirical testing. The primary goal is to develop and evaluate an efficient, real-time traffic safety system for school zones using advanced sensor technology and edge computing.
The system design phase involved creating a scalable and modular architecture capable of handling high-volume, real-time data streams from multiple sensors. We implemented a three-tier edge computing model: Sensor-edge for initial camera data processing, near-edge for intermediate LiDAR data computations, and cloud-edge for data fusion and decision-making.
Sensor integration was a crucial aspect of our methodology. Each sensor is connected to the network via a dedicated 5G router, enabling low-latency data transmission. For data processing, we employed state-of-the-art algorithms for object detection and tracking. At the sensor-edge level, we utilized an object detection algorithm provided by our project partner, Peregrine Technologies, for initial object detection in camera feeds. For LiDAR data, we implemented a point cloud processing pipeline on the near-edge servers, using clustering algorithms for object segmentation and a deep learning-based approach for object classification.
Data collection methods were designed to ensure comprehensive environmental monitoring. We deployed an array of five LiDAR sensors and five high-resolution cameras, strategically positioned to cover the two target school zone areas. Each sensor is equipped with time synchronization mechanisms, including GPS for LiDAR and Network Time Protocol (NTP) for the near-edge servers, to ensure accurate timestamp alignment across the system.
Our analytical approach focuses on assessing system performance across several key metrics: data transmission latency, packet loss rates, object detection accuracy, and decision-making speed. We developed custom software tools and utilized existing network analysis tools like iperf3 [23] to measure these metrics under various operational conditions.
To evaluate the system’s effectiveness in real-world scenarios, we conducted a series of field tests designed to assess the system’s ability to detect and track vehicles and pedestrians, analyze traffic patterns, and generate timely safety alerts under different traffic conditions. Throughout the research process, we adhered to strict ethical guidelines, ensuring all data collection and processing methods complied with relevant privacy regulations.
4.2 Experimental studies
The following experimental studies, summarized in Table 1, provide an evaluation of the hardware and software components, measurement setups, and contextual parameters utilized in our 5-Safe system.
4.2.1 Study 1: UDP transmission from LiDAR to near-edge
Study 1.1: UDP Packet Transmission Latency The primary objective of this study is to measure the latency or delay between the collection of data by LiDAR and its arrival at the near-edge device. This involves precise synchronization of both the LiDAR and the server to ensure accurate measurement of the time difference between these two events.
To accurately measure the latency two specific timestamps are required:
-
Timestamp 1 (\({t}_{\text {device}}\)): The time, when the LiDAR transmits point cloud data.
-
Timestamp 2 (\({t}_{\text {host}}\)): The time, when the transmitted point cloud data arrives at the near-edge.
The difference between these two timestamps will provide the desired latency measurement for the UDP transmission, provided the clocks of the LiDAR and near-edge are synchronized.
In the 5-Safe project, synchronization of the LiDAR is achieved by integrating a GPS hardware module. This module ensures that the LiDAR data is synchronized with a precise GPS-supplied time signal. By synchronizing the LiDAR to GPS time, we ensure that the exact firing moment of each data point is captured accurately.
On the near-edge side, synchronization is achieved through the setup of an NTP server. This server is configured to synchronize with the same GPS-supplied time as the LiDAR to ensure both systems are operating in synchronization (Sync.).
-
NTP Server Setup: The GPU server runs a NTP client that synchronizes with GPS time, ensuring that all timestamps it generates are aligned with those produced by the LiDAR sensors.
-
Time Synchronization: By aligning both the LiDAR and GPU server with the GPS time, we ensure that the two systems can measure the transmission latency with minimal time drift or skew.
The latency between the LiDAR and GPU is computed using Eq. (1):
This latency \(T_{L_ec}\) represents the time it takes for the point cloud data to travel from the LiDAR to the near-edge via UDP. \(T_{L_p}\) is the sum of \(T_{L_ec}\) and the processing time on near-edge.
Study 1.2: UDP Packet Transmission Loss The goal of this study is to evaluate the potential packet loss during the transmission of UDP packets from LiDAR sensors to the near-edge (GPU server). Given that UDP does not guarantee delivery, measuring packet loss is essential for understanding the limitations of the transmission method, particularly in time-sensitive applications like object detection and tracking. To measure the UDP packet transmission loss, we utilized iperf3, a widely-used tool for network performance testing.
Our real-world scenario enables the transmission of point cloud data from LiDAR sensors to the GPU server over a network configuration, allowing us to analyze potential packet loss under deployment conditions. We set up a sender system to control the LiDAR sensor, configured to generate and send UDP packets at 5 frames per second, matching our actual LiDAR data output rate. The receiver system on our GPU server responsible for processing the incoming data.
In this real-world setup, we connected the LiDAR system to a 5G router in school zones, in addition we protect data via VPN during transmission to the GPU server.
For this scenario, we monitored key metrics, including data sent, data received, and the ratio of lost to total datagrams. The packet loss rate is calculated using the following formula Eq. (2):
where \( P_{L_R} \) in % represents the packet loss rate, \( P_s \) is the total number of packets sent, and \( P_r \) is the total number of packets received.
These measurements provide critical insights into the reliability of our data transmission under network conditions. Understanding packet loss is particularly important for the 5-Safe system, as missing data could lead to gaps in environmental monitoring and potentially affect the accuracy of our safety alerts. By quantifying packet loss, we aim to optimize our system’s performance and reliability.
4.2.2 Study 2: Execution latency on near-edge (object detection, fusion and tracking)
The aim of this study is to evaluate the execution latency of object detection, sensor fusion, and tracking processes on the near-edge GPU server.
Our objective is to measure the latency from the moment a packet is received from multiple LiDAR sensors to the time when processed results become available after object detection, fusion, and tracking.
While ground truth validation is conducted to confirm the correct operation of detection and tracking algorithms, the primary focus of this paper is on the timeliness of data transmission and processing across the system pipeline. We do not report precision, recall, or accuracy metrics for detection and tracking, as these aspects have been validated separately and are out of the scope of this work. All latency and transmission measurements presented here assume that the underlying detection and tracking modules function correctly, as verified by prior ground truth validation.
This analysis is crucial for optimizing our real-time traffic safety application, where timely processing of sensor data is essential for rapid response to potential hazards in school zones.
To assess the execution latency on the near-edge, we implemented a system that captures two key time points:
-
Reception Time: The time, when a data packet is received from the LiDAR sensors on the GPU server.
-
Post-Processing Time: The time, when the object detection, fusion, and tracking processes are completed on the near-edge node.
By measuring the time difference, we determined the near-edge processing latency, offering insight into end-to-end execution from data acquisition to fused tracking output.
Our measurement setup utilized a multi-LiDAR configuration mirroring the 5-Safe system deployment in the school zone.
UDP packets containing point cloud data are sent from each LiDAR to the GPU server, triggering a series of processing functions:
-
Object Detection: Point cloud data from each LiDAR is processed to identify and localize objects within the 3D environment.
-
Sensor Fusion: Data from multiple LiDARs is combined to create a unified view, enhancing accuracy and reducing redundancy.
-
Tracking: Detected objects are monitored across frames to maintain a consistent representation of their movement over time.
During testing, we collected time measurements for both packet reception and the completion of GPU-based processing under varying network conditions.
This approach allowed us to understand the impact of network latency and GPU load on the overall processing time, reflecting real-world execution timings for our multi-LiDAR configuration.
4.2.3 Study 3: Benchmark between MQTT, Kafka, and TCP socket
The choice of messaging protocol plays a critical role in determining the performance and QoS of a system. Specifically, guaranteeing packet transmission within a specified deadline is essential for our 5-Safe system as a real-time smart-city application.
In this study, we present a comprehensive benchmark analysis of various communication protocols, specifically MQTT, Kafka, and Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) sockets, focusing on evaluating key performance metrics such as throughput and latency.
The goal is to identify which protocol can meet stringent transmission deadlines while maintaining optimal QoS in our smart city use case. MQTT, widely adopted in Internet of Things (IoT) applications, excels in lightweight messaging, whereas Kafka is designed for high-throughput scenarios, especially in real-time data processing. TCP provides reliable communication. This study presents a detailed analysis of the performance characteristics of these protocols.
MQTT is optimized for use in low-bandwidth, high-latency networks, making it particularly suitable for IoT applications. Its lightweight overhead facilitates efficient communication in constrained environments. Performance evaluations typically focus on latency and message delivery efficiency, with benchmarking methodologies assessing its real-time suitability in various IoT use cases [24].
Kafka is engineered to support high-throughput, fault-tolerant messaging systems, outperforming other protocols in scenarios that demand real-time data processing and long-term system stability [25]. Kafka is particularly advantageous in large-scale data pipelines and stream processing, where its capacity to handle vast amounts of data with low latency proves critical [26].
TCP socket, as a fundamental transport layer protocol, ensures reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of data packets. However, it lacks built-in mechanisms for message queuing, making it less suitable for scenarios involving message brokers like MQTT and Kafka. Despite this, TCP serves as the foundational communication layer for these protocols, underscoring its importance in ensuring network reliability.
To summarize, while MQTT and Kafka are tailored to specific communication requirements, particularly in IoT applications, TCP socket’s role is more foundational, supporting the underlying communication infrastructure. Additionally, we did not include stateless push-based architectures such as direct HTTP/WebSocket-based messaging. These may exhibit lower initial latency in controlled settings but lack resilience, fault-tolerance, and message ordering guarantees. In future comparative studies, we plan to include these push protocols to assess trade-offs between architectural simplicity and system reliability in dynamic environments.
Therefore, selecting the appropriate protocol depends on the specific application requirements, including factors such as scalability, latency tolerance, and throughput needs. We evaluate these protocols in real-world scenarios based on the aforementioned studies to determine which best suits our solution. Based on experimental results, Kafka outperforms MQTT and TCP sockets in terms of meeting the required transmission deadlines and maintaining higher overall efficiency.
4.2.4 Study 4: Data transmission from near-edge to cloud-edge through 5G
This study aims to measure the latency experienced during the transmission of object list from the near-edge to the cloud-edge via Kafka. This latency is crucial for understanding the real-time performance of the system, especially in applications that require timely responses to road conditions. Therefore, to accurately measure the link latency, both the GPU server and the cloud-edge node are synchronized to the same time source using an NTP server. This ensures that the timestamps logged on both systems are consistent and comparable.
Near-Edge: When the list of detected object is generated on the GPU server, a timestamp is recorded at the moment the data is passed to the Kafka producer. This timestamp is denoted as \({t}_{p}\) (Kafka producing time).
Cloud-Edge: When the object list data is received and consumed by the Kafka consumer on the Edge node, another timestamp is recorded, denoted as \({t}_{c}\) (Kafka consuming time). Ultimately, the latency for data transmission through Kafka is calculated by comparing the two timestamps Eq. (3):
This latency represents the time needed for data transmission from the GPU Server to the cloud-edge node via Kafka, including the network transmission time and Kafka processing overhead.
4.2.5 Study 5: Execution latency of camera object detection and transmission to cloud-edge
In this study, we examined the execution latency of critical processes in our edge computing infrastructure, focusing on camera-based object detection (\(T_{C_p}\)).
We used timing mechanisms synchronized across our edge computing infrastructure to ensure accurate latency measurements. In addition, we conducted multiple iterations to account for variability and to establish confidence intervals for our latency metric.
Our camera system utilizes Jetson Xavier NX devices for running the object detection algorithm of Peregrine [27] on the incoming video frames to identify and localize vehicles, pedestrians, and other relevant objects in the school zone. The data collected by the object detection model is transmitted as a JSON-formatted object list over a 5G network to the cloud-edge device using a Kafka producer. These devices form a privacy-preserving video-based network capable of traffic monitoring and rapid response to traffic events, particularly during school start and end times.
The latency is influenced by several factors, including object detection processing time (\(T_{C_p}\)), data transmission using Kafka producers, and 5G network transmission to the cloud-edge device (\(T_{C_c}\)). While 5G network transmission latency can be as low as a few milliseconds under ideal conditions, practical latency may fluctuate due to network conditions.
4.2.6 Study 6: Execution latency on cloud-edge
In this study, we measure the execution delay inside the cloud-edge cluster (\(T_E\)). Focusing primarily on communication delays, which emerged as the dominant factor affecting the system’s end-to-end responsiveness. While container execution times were monitored using Jaeger tracing, the measured delays were consistently minimal. Although we did not examine these execution times in our performance evaluation, as they were not central to the problem under investigation. Our analysis, therefore, prioritizes communication latency, which poses greater challenges for meeting real-time requirements in the 5-Safe system.
5 Experimental results
In this section, we outline our experimental setup, evaluate the real-world performance of the 5-Safe solution. The following experimental studies focus exclusively on measuring system latency and data transmission characteristics. This paper does not include precision or accuracy metrics, as our primary objective is to assess the system’s real-time performance. We also address the challenges of ensuring a deterministic and dependable system, highlighting critical aspects needed to enhance reliability and predictability.
5.1 Experimental setup
Figure 3 illustrates the setup for our real-world experimental studies. The Kubernetes cluster is installed on the cloud-edge server in Munich. The cloud-edge infrastructure used for experimental evaluation was deployed on an Ubuntu 20.04 environment, with containerized services orchestrated using K8s version 1.23.17 and Docker Engine version 20.10.18. This configuration was selected to ensure compatibility with Docker-based workloads and to reflect realistic edge computing environments. All microservices, including data synchronization, fusion, and decision-making, were deployed as individual containers managed by the Kubernetes cluster. This setup enables reproducibility and stable container orchestration during system performance testing.
The sensors are equipped at the front of schools in the City of Landshut. In addition, the near-edge resource for LiDAR sensor data is established at Landshut’s innovation center LINK. The cameras and LiDARs are linked to the GPU server, and the cloud-edge server is interconnected through a 5G wireless network.
The used setup closely mirrors the system architecture (Fig. 1). Each measured component, including sensor data acquisition, edge processing, synchronization, and decision forwarding, corresponds directly to a specific module in the architecture. This ensures that the latency and performance results reflect real-world operating conditions.
The Velodyne VLP-32C LiDAR is utilized, equipped with a pre-configured Garmin 18x LVC GPS receiver (P/N 80-GPS18LVC) to ensure precise time synchronization of the LiDAR’s data capture. The Velodyne VLP-32C features a horizontal field of view of ±360° and a vertical field of view of \(\pm 40\)°, allowing for comprehensive environmental scanning. The used LiDAR can measure distances up to 200 ms with a point cloud density of up to 1.3 million points per second, making it suitable for high-resolution mapping applications.
The GPU server is configured with an open NTP server ("time.nist.gov"), which relies on GPS time to synchronize timestamps with the LiDAR. The computational power for processing the LiDAR sensor data is provided by a dual NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 setup, each equipped with 24 GB of GDDR6X memory and capable of handling complex computations efficiently. The GPUs were housed in a server featuring an AMD EPYC 7543 processor, which has 32 cores operating at a base clock speed of 2.8 GHz and can boost up to 3.7 GHz, ensuring robust performance during data processing tasks.
The GPU server is equipped with 128 GB of DDR4-3200 ECC RAM, facilitating smooth multitasking and efficient data handling. For storage, we used two U.2 NVMe SSDs: one with a capacity of 960 GB and another with 1.9 TB, both designed for continuous operation (24×7) and rated for one drive write per day (DWPD), ensuring reliable data logging for the study.
Data were collected continuously for 24 h, during which both the device timestamp (from the LiDAR) and the host timestamp (from the GPU server) were recorded. These timestamps allow us to calculate the latency for each UDP packet by taking the difference between the host timestamp and the device timestamp.
Given these considerations, and in compliance with privacy constraints and data protection regulations in school zones, we are currently unable to publish raw video outputs or annotated frames. Nevertheless, internal validation sessions conducted with authorized personnel have confirmed the system’s detection accuracy during live traffic operation. Future publications will include anonymized visual samples or heatmap overlays to illustrate detection consistency without compromising data privacy.
5.2 Results
5.2.1 UDP transmission from LiDAR to near-edge (Study 1)
The latency \(T_{L_c}\) is analyzed in 4-h intervals. For each interval, we computed the maximum and average latency. Additionally, packet loss is evaluated to understand the reliability of the UDP transmission under varying conditions. These values provide insights into the performance consistency of UDP packet transmission.
Figure 4 shows significant variability in maximum latency between different LiDARs and time intervals. For example:
-
LiDAR3 shows a maximum latency of 700 ms between 8:00 AM and noon, which then decreases to 400–500 ms in other intervals. Its average latency of 52 ms aligns with the expected soft real-time constraints [28].
-
LiDAR2 peaks at 800 ms in the 4:00 PM to 8:00 PM interval, dropping to 450 ms in others and averaging 57 ms.
-
LiDAR1 records a maximum latency of 700 ms between 8:00 p.m. and midnight, with lower maxima in other intervals and an average latency of 52 ms.
This variability indicates that while average latencies (Fig. 5) generally meet soft real-time requirements, occasional spikes may challenge latency constraints, particularly when predictable response times are essential. Further optimization of UDP packet transmission is necessary to minimize transmission delays and meet hard real-time requirements. In addition, packet loss (Fig. 6) is measured to assess the reliability of UDP transmission. A brief but notable spike in packet loss is observed at the beginning of the measurement, likely caused by transient network congestion during system initialization, such as temporary instability in the 5G link or VPN tunnel. Since this spike occurs only during the initial seconds and does not reflect steady-state system performance, it is excluded from our average and subsequent analysis. The results indicate that the average packet loss remained around 18%, with occasional spikes above 20% during peak times, particularly in areas with fluctuating 5G coverage. This consistent packet loss suggests that a significant portion of data packets may not reach the near edge (GPU server), potentially compromising object detection and tracking accuracy. High packet loss rates could lead to incomplete or delayed data processing, affecting the system’s ability to provide timely safety alerts. The latency variability, which can reach 800 ms, along with packet loss rates, could affect the dependability of the 5-Safe system to meet timing requirements, particularly for real-time decision-making. Although most LiDARs maintain an average latency below 58 ms, the observed spikes indicate potential bottlenecks that could delay critical responses, such as emergency traffic warnings. Addressing these challenges may require further analysis to optimize network paths or adopt protocols with better latency guarantees. Nevertheless, the system is designed to degrade gracefully: in the event of a dropped frame, the tracker interpolates the object’s last known state using temporal continuity, ensuring uninterrupted safety decisions even under lossy conditions. This study lays the foundation for future research and system improvements aimed at predictable low-latency data transmission in safety-critical applications. The insights from these latency patterns and packet loss measurements can guide design adjustments or protocol enhancements to reduce spikes and ensure reliable data delivery. By establishing benchmarks and identifying factors that influence UDP performance, this research supports the advancement of next-generation CPS and smart city applications where deterministic performance is vital. The empirical validation of UDP transmission latencies and packet loss highlights areas where metrics exceed soft real-time constraints, providing guidance for future enhancements to achieve deterministic timing requirements in resource-constrained embedded systems and CPS.
5.2.2 Execution latency on near-edge (Study 2)
Figure 7 illustrates the time delay \(T_{L_p}\) for processing LiDAR object detection, tracking, and fusion tasks on a GPU-based system over a 24-h period, measured at 4-h intervals. The average processing time across all intervals is approximately 0.0544 s, with a peak processing time of 0.057 observed between 8:00 AM and 12:00 PM. The data shows slight variability, with higher processing times during peak intervals (8:00 AM–12:00 PM and 4:00 PM–8:00 PM) compared to other day times. Despite these fluctuations, the processing times remain within an acceptable range, demonstrating the GPU capability to handle real-time LiDAR processing requirements efficiently. The test is conducted using optimized GPU hardware to ensure that data processing remains timely and effective for real-time applications.
5.2.3 Benchmarking MQTT, Kafka, and TCP socket (Study 3)
Figure 9 presents the real-world benchmarked transmission delays of TCP socket, Kafka, and MQTT protocols over varying object sizes (1 KB, 100 KB, and 1 MB). Kafka generally demonstrates lower transmission delays than MQTT, particularly for smaller object sizes, making it well-suited for real-time applications requiring rapid data exchange. However, when transmitting larger data volumes, such as an object list of 1 MB size, MQTT shows a slight advantage in reducing transmission delays. This confirms MQTT efficiency in handling larger packets under specific conditions.
Figure 10 illustrates the packet loss percentages for data transmission using Kafka and MQTT across different object list sizes. While MQTT demonstrates a slight advantage in reducing delay for larger files (as seen in Fig. 9 ), Kafka exhibits consistently lower packet loss rates across all data sizes. For instance, when transmitting a 1 MB object list, Kafka experienced only 6.8% packet loss, compared to 25% packet loss using MQTT under the same conditions.
This indicates Kafka’s robustness and reliability in ensuring data integrity, which is particularly important in applications where data loss could impact critical functions. Kafka’s stability in minimizing packet loss even as data size increases makes it a strong candidate for our use cases, where high data fidelity is paramount.
This raises an intriguing question about the potential of MQTT as a real-time protocol while ensuring deterministic guarantees. As Kafka provides reliable QoS across various data sizes, an enhanced, real-time-optimized version of MQTT could be particularly advantageous for scenarios requiring both low latency and scalability in handling larger data transmissions. Further research into developing a guaranteed real-time MQTT variant–optimized for both delay and packet management could address this emerging need, especially in high-demand IoT applications.
5.2.4 Transmission delay from near-edge to cloud-edge (Study 4)
Figure 8 illustrates the transmission time (\(T_{G_c}\)) between the near-edge and the cloud-edge node through the 5G network. The measurement is performed over 24 h, where the data is collected every 4 h. The results indicate relatively low and consistent transmission delays, averaging between 0.01 and 0.07 sec. However, slightly higher delays were observed during peak hours, particularly between 8:00 AM–12:00 PM and 4:00 PM–8:00 PM, where the transmission times occasionally approached the upper range of 0.07 s. Despite these minor increases, the transmission times remained stable overall, indicating that the network infrastructure is capable of supporting the real-time data transmission requirements of the 5-Safe system, ensuring prompt delivery of detected object data for decision-making at the cloud-edge. Yet, we recognize that Kafka performance over extended timeframes (beyond 24 h) was not evaluated in this study. In subsequent deployments, we aim to conduct long-range monitoring to observe memory management behavior, broker log retention policies, and throughput degradation. These insights will help evaluate Kafka’s long-term suitability for continuous traffic safety monitoring.
5.2.5 Execution latency of camera object detection and transmission to cloud-edge (Study 5)
Our comprehensive latency analysis reveals a systematic increase in overall system latency, which encompasses both object detection time (\(T_{C_p}\)) and the transmission of object lists through Kafka over a real 5G network (\(T_{C_c}\)), as illustrated in Fig. 11. This increase can be primarily attributed to consumer lag, a phenomenon that occurs when consumers are unable to keep pace with the rate at which messages are produced. As a result, there is a growing discrepancy between the timestamps of produced and consumed messages.
Several factors contribute to this consumer lag. One significant cause is high incoming traffic; during periods of sudden surges in message volume, consumers may struggle to process data quickly enough, leading to increased latency. Additionally, slow processing within consumer applications can further exacerbate this issue. If these applications are hampered by inefficient processing logic or experience performance bottlenecks, they may fall behind in their ability to consume messages effectively.
The implications of this latency are critical, as it challenges the near real-time response necessary for managing traffic events and making rapid adjustments to signal control. Such responsiveness is essential for maintaining safety in school zones.
Our latency analysis reveals that lag is primarily bursty rather than persistent under sustained load. This behavior is likely due to queue spikes triggered by synchronized sensor activity during peak traffic hours. As a mitigation strategy, we plan to introduce Kafka topic partitioning and consumer-side backpressure mechanisms to improve load balancing and reduce end-to-end latency peaks in future deployments.
5.3 Discussion
The 5-Safe system research offers a comprehensive real-world evaluation of a multi-tier edge computing architecture for traffic safety in school zones. Our analysis revealed substantial variability in UDP transmission latency. The observed latency spikes (up to 800 ms) and packet loss (18–20%) reveal that while the system performs adequately for firm real-time applications, where occasional delays are tolerable, it fails to meet the stringent latency requirements for hard real-time systems (≤50 ms).
Despite these challenges, the system’s performance remains acceptable for scenarios where firm real-time is sufficient, but to meet the demands of hard real-time systems, where strict latency guarantees are critical, therefore, implementing resource reservation mechanisms becomes essential to meet the stringent demands of hard real-time systems, where strict latency guarantees are non-negotiable. Such strategies can ensure predictable performance by pre-allocating network and compute resources, thereby minimizing the impact of variability and delay [13]. Kafka proved effective for streaming small payloads and maintaining time-sensitive data flow. However, consumer lag and decision-message propagation delays indicate that further optimization is necessary. Additionally, our study does not directly evaluate pedestrian risk reduction or simulate near-miss scenarios; it rigorously assesses the system’s timeliness and responsiveness in real-world deployment. We demonstrate that the 5-Safe system can reliably detect and process potential hazards with minimal latency using public 5G infrastructure, which is a critical prerequisite for any effective safety intervention. These results establish the technical foundation for future studies that will focus on quantifying actual risk reduction and system effectiveness in preventing incidents. These findings reveal critical bottlenecks and offer a foundation for improving the determinism and reliability of edge-based cyber-physical safety systems.
Ultimately, validating 5-Safe as a safety intervention system requires the measurement of ROC curves, intervention rates, and missed detections. In the current deployment phase, such metrics cannot be captured due to the absence of labeled ground truth in real-time operation. Future evaluations will address this limitation by employing video annotation frameworks and incident simulations to derive true positive, false positive, and miss rates, thereby enabling a quantitative assessment of the system’s intervention effectiveness.
6 Conclusion and future work
This study advances the state of the art in real-time, multi-tier edge computing for urban traffic safety. Through a rigorous, real-world evaluation, we have identified key performance characteristics and practical challenges in deploying such systems at scale. Our results provide a foundation for future research and development, moving closer to intelligent, responsive urban infrastructures capable of protecting vulnerable populations and optimizing mobility in complex environments.
Future work should target enhanced network optimization, resilient transmission methods, and intelligent protocol selection. Moreover, integrating predictive machine learning models and next-generation real-time communication protocols could significantly improve latency bounds and decision accuracy [29]. In addition, real urban networks frequently experience congestion and failover events that can significantly affect communication reliability and latency. Future research should explicitly simulate these conditions to evaluate and compare the resilience and robustness of messaging protocols under realistic network stress scenarios. These efforts will be crucial for evolving toward robust, scalable smart city infrastructure that safeguards vulnerable populations and supports context-aware mobility.
Data availability
Due to privacy protection policies and data confidentiality regulations in school zones, raw video footage and LiDAR data cannot be publicly released. However, anonymized performance metrics, latency benchmarks, and protocol configurations are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
